question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user forge the X-Real-IP header with an arbitrary value to spoof their source identity in all audit logs, allowing them to conduct Hashgraph history tampering requests (e.g., crafted block/transaction queries) while the logs attribute the activity to a trusted internal IP, eliminating forensic traceability?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set the X-Forwarded-For header to a spoofed IP address, bypassing any IP-allowlist or rate-limit enforcement that relies on the logged client IP, thereby enabling repeated Hashgraph history manipulation requests without triggering IP-based blocking or alerting?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a multi-value X-Forwarded-For header (e.g., '10.0.0.1, 192.168.1.1') to confuse downstream log parsers into attributing Hashgraph history tampering requests to a trusted internal address, causing security monitoring to miss the attack entirely?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user inject newline characters (\\n, \\r\\n) into the X-Real-IP header value to perform log injection, forging fake log entries that show legitimate Hashgraph history reads while hiding actual tampering requests in the same log stream?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user inject newline characters into the X-Forwarded-For header to insert fabricated log lines (e.g., fake 200 OK responses for block endpoints) into the Rosetta audit log, causing operators to believe Hashgraph history is intact when it has been queried or manipulated?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request path containing newline characters (via URL encoding such as %0a or %0d%0a) that, when logged via fmt.Sprintf in TracingMiddleware, injects fake log entries attributing Hashgraph history tampering to a different IP or endpoint, corrupting the audit trail?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a path that includes ANSI escape sequences or terminal control characters, which when written to log output via log.Info(message) corrupt log file integrity, making it impossible to reconstruct the sequence of Hashgraph history access events?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware logs the full request.URL.RequestURI() (including query parameters) to inject structured log fields (e.g., JSON key-value pairs) into the log message, causing log aggregation systems to misparse Hashgraph history access records and hide tampering activity?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a Hashgraph history endpoint (e.g., /block) with a path crafted to exactly match an entry in internalPaths (e.g., via path traversal or normalization bypass), causing TracingMiddleware to log the request at debug level instead of info level, making the tampering attempt invisible in production log monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the internalPaths map lookup (internalPaths[path]) by crafting a URL whose RequestURI() resolves to /health/liveness, /health/readiness, or /metrics through URL normalization differences, causing a Hashgraph history probe to be silently downgraded to debug-level logging and evade detection?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the Rosetta API with high-volume requests to Hashgraph history endpoints (e.g., /block/transaction), causing TracingMiddleware to generate unbounded log output via log.Info(message) that exhausts disk space, making the node unable to record further events and creating a window for undetected history tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.Write()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a response from a Hashgraph history endpoint that returns an extremely large payload, causing tracingResponseWriter.Write() to store the entire response body in w.data ([]byte), exhausting heap memory and crashing the Rosetta node, thereby preventing legitimate Hashgraph history verification?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.Write()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly trigger large responses from Hashgraph history endpoints, causing the w.data field in tracingResponseWriter to accumulate across concurrent requests, leading to memory exhaustion that degrades or crashes the node and prevents detection of ongoing history tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: tracingResponseWriter.WriteHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that causes the inner handler to call WriteHeader() multiple times (e.g., via a redirect chain), causing tracingResponseWriter.statusCode to be overwritten with an incorrect value, resulting in the logged status code misrepresenting the actual outcome of a Hashgraph history tampering attempt?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: newTracingResponseWriter()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that newTracingResponseWriter initializes statusCode to http.StatusOK (200) regardless of the actual response, so that if the inner handler panics or fails to call WriteHeader(), the logged status code falsely shows 200 for a failed Hashgraph history request, masking errors that indicate tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user set both X-Real-IP and X-Forwarded-For headers simultaneously, exploiting the priority logic (X-Real-IP checked first) to ensure a spoofed IP is always used in logs, while the actual attacker IP in X-Forwarded-For is ignored, permanently hiding the true source of Hashgraph history tampering requests?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user send an empty X-Real-IP header and a crafted X-Forwarded-For header containing a trusted internal IP (e.g., 127.0.0.1), causing getClientIpAddress() to return the spoofed internal IP and log Hashgraph history tampering requests as originating from localhost, bypassing IP-based security controls?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an X-Real-IP header with an IPv6 address formatted to confuse log parsers (e.g., '::1' or '0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1'), causing the logged IP to appear as localhost and making Hashgraph history tampering requests appear to originate from the server itself?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with an extremely long path string (e.g., thousands of characters) to Hashgraph history endpoints, causing fmt.Sprintf in TracingMiddleware to produce oversized log lines that overflow log buffer limits, truncating or corrupting adjacent log entries and obscuring evidence of history tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the timing measurement (time.Since(start)) in TracingMiddleware to perform a timing side-channel attack, inferring whether specific Hashgraph block or transaction records exist in the database based on response latency differences logged for different block indices?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high volume of concurrent requests to Hashgraph history endpoints, causing TracingMiddleware to spawn goroutines that each allocate a tracingResponseWriter, exhausting goroutine/memory resources and causing the Rosetta node to become unresponsive, preventing legitimate history verification?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request where request.Method contains special characters or is unusually long, causing the fmt.Sprintf log message to include attacker-controlled content that, when parsed by log aggregation tools, creates false records of Hashgraph history access patterns?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: getClientIpAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an X-Forwarded-For header with a list of IPs (e.g., 'attacker_ip, trusted_ip') where the last IP is a trusted address, exploiting the fact that getClientIpAddress() uses the entire header value without parsing individual IPs, causing the full string to be logged and confusing IP-based alerting for Hashgraph history tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests to Hashgraph history endpoints with paths that contain null bytes (%00), causing the path logged by TracingMiddleware to be truncated in certain log storage systems, hiding the true endpoint being accessed and masking history tampering probes?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/trace.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: TracingMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TracingMiddleware calls inner.ServeHTTP before logging, meaning that if the inner handler modifies Hashgraph state and then panics, the panic is not caught by TracingMiddleware, resulting in no log entry for the tampering request and leaving no audit trail?
